character.name: Arleen Belle
character.source_citation: Desmond, Matthew. Evicted: Poverty and Profit in the American City. Crown, 2016.
character.situation: A mother of two boys facing serial eviction in Milwaukee. Each eviction triggers a cascade — new school, lost job, broken social connections, deeper poverty. The eviction record itself becomes a barrier to future housing.
character.full_landscape: A mother whose primary project is keeping her family together and housed. Resourceful, strategic, deeply knowledgeable about landlord-tenant dynamics. Her 'problem' is a housing market designed to extract from her, not serve her.
character.production_challenge: The dome must address the eviction cascade — how one housing loss triggers failures across employment, education, healthcare, and social connection. The financial instruments need to make coordination cheaper than fragmentation.

stage_log.focus: Map the legal landscape. Discover every entitlement. Model every system.

deliverables.title: Character Documentary Treatment: Andre Washington
deliverables.description: Drawing on "Docket", Andre Washington builds the documentary treatment around Arleen Belle's full landscape: A mother whose primary project is keeping her family together and housed. Resourceful, strategic, deeply knowledgeable a. The treatment refuses to flatten Arleen Belle into a case study. The camera doesn't have an opinion, but it has a position. The narrative structure mirrors the fragmentation — the audience experiences the same disorientation of navigating 7 disconnected systems, then the relief when coordination begins to work. [Ryan Coogler's direction: Community-rooted world-building.]

work_referenced.title: Docket
work_referenced.description: Feature documentary on eviction courts, premiered at Sundance, cited in 4 legislative hearings

work_referenced.title: Courtroom Portraits
work_referenced.description: Photography series documenting the physical spaces of housing justice

deliverables.title: Systems Fragmentation Model: Dr. Kenji Nakamura
deliverables.description: Extending "Cascade: Hospital Readmission Feedback Loops" to Arleen Belle's situation, Dr. Kenji Nakamura models the cascade: a single failure in Medicaid propagates through Medicaid, compounding costs at each handoff. The model quantifies the fragmentation tax — the hours, the duplicate applications, the missed deadlines that aren't bugs but features of systems designed to operate in isolation. Every system failure has a signature — a pattern of cascading consequences that you can map, model, and predict. The simulation shows that coordinating just 1 of 7 systems would reduce the administrative burden on Arleen Belle by the equivalent of a part-time job. [Ryan Coogler's direction: Community-rooted world-building.]

work_referenced.title: Cascade: Hospital Readmission Feedback Loops
work_referenced.description: 6-year longitudinal study revealing how single-system failures propagate across healthcare, housing, and income

work_referenced.title: The Fragmentation Index
work_referenced.description: Open-source model quantifying the cost of non-coordination across government programs

deliverables.title: Flourishing Dimensions Analysis: Amara Okafor
deliverables.description: Using "Roots Kitchen Network" as foundation, Amara Okafor maps Arleen Belle's specific flourishing dimensions: housing, employment, education, community, dignity, stability, financial security. Starting with stability, the analysis asks: what does stability actually look like for Arleen Belle — not in the abstract, but on a Tuesday morning? A kitchen is the most honest room in any building. The framework maps each dimension against current system coverage and finds that public schools addresses stability partially but ignores stability entirely. [Ryan Coogler's direction: Community-rooted world-building.]

work_referenced.title: Roots Kitchen Network
work_referenced.description: 7 community kitchens across South Side Chicago functioning as neighborhood living rooms

work_referenced.title: Season Tables
work_referenced.description: Cookbook and social practice guide connecting seasonal eating to community gathering rhythms

deliverables.title: Legal Landscape Map: Patricia Hawkins-Morrow
deliverables.description: Applying "The Architecture of Rights" to Arleen Belle's case, Patricia Hawkins-Morrow identifies that Arleen Belle intersects with 7 systems simultaneously — focusing first on Medicaid. The gap analysis reveals that eligibility exists on paper across all systems, but application timelines conflict and documentation requirements between Medicaid and adjacent systems duplicate effort without sharing data. A right on paper and a right in practice are two different buildings. The map exposes 7 specific coordination failures that, if resolved, would unlock dignity simultaneously. [Ryan Coogler's direction: Community-rooted world-building.]

work_referenced.title: The Architecture of Rights
work_referenced.description: Book analyzing how legal entitlements function as structural supports or structural failures

work_referenced.title: Rights Gap Analysis Tool
work_referenced.description: Open-source tool for identifying gaps between legal entitlements and actual access

deliverables.title: Public Voice Installation: Zara Kalil
deliverables.description: Nobody put public art on the production plan for this dome.. But Zara Kalil brings "The Unofficial History Project" — 40+ large-scale murals documenting community histories in neighborhoods facing gentrification. Applied to Arleen Belle's dome, their practice reframes education as something the other disciplines couldn't see. A wall is either talking or it's silent. Their methodology from "Wall Rights" — Legal advocacy for artists' rights to public expression, established precedent i — produces an entirely new category of output for Arleen Belle's dome. This is the collision that makes the portfolio undeniable.

work_referenced.title: Wall Rights
work_referenced.description: Legal advocacy for artists' rights to public expression, established precedent in 3 cities

deliverables.title: dome Nourishment Protocol: Santiago Reyes-Moreno
deliverables.description: Nobody put brownfield remediation on the production plan for this dome.. But Santiago Reyes-Moreno brings "Remediation Gardens" — 14 brownfield-to-garden conversions across 3 cities, $2.3M annual food production. Applied to Arleen Belle's dome, their practice reframes employment as something the other disciplines couldn't see. Every vacant lot has a memory in its soil. Their methodology from "Soil Memory" — Research on how industrial contamination histories affect community relationship — produces an entirely new category of output for Arleen Belle's dome. This is the collision that makes the portfolio undeniable.

deliverables.title: Production Narrative Framework: Andre Washington
deliverables.description: From the treatment, Andre Washington architects the production narrative: how Arleen Belle's story moves through the dome pipeline. Drawing on "Docket", the framework structures the dome not as a rescue story but as an engineering problem — 7 systems that should work together and don't, and what it takes to make them. The camera doesn't have an opinion, but it has a position. The narrative architecture gives equal weight to Arleen Belle's intelligence, humor, and agency as it does to the systems that constrain them. [Ryan Coogler's direction: Community-rooted world-building.]

deliverables.title: Cost-of-Fragmentation Model: Dr. Kenji Nakamura
deliverables.description: From the fragmentation model, Dr. Kenji Nakamura builds the cost comparison: what Arleen Belle's case costs under current fragmentation vs. what it would cost under coordination. Extending "Cascade: Hospital Readmission Feedback Loops", the model shows that public schools and adjacent systems alone generate duplicate administrative costs — the same information collected twice, stored in incompatible formats, verified by different standards. Every system failure has a signature — a pattern of cascading consequences that you can map, model, and predict. The cost-of-fragmentation model makes the invisible tax visible: Arleen Belle is paying for government's failure to coordinate, in hours that could go toward stability. [Ryan Coogler's direction: Community-rooted world-building.]

deliverables.title: Dome Architecture Concept: Amara Okafor
deliverables.description: From the dimensions analysis, Amara Okafor designs the dome's architecture: a spatial and conceptual environment where Arleen Belle's 7 flourishing dimensions are structurally supported. Drawing on "Roots Kitchen Network", the concept treats every transition between housing court and adjacent systems as a design problem — not a bureaucratic one. A kitchen is the most honest room in any building. The dome's architecture ensures that pursuing housing never requires sacrificing stability. The systems serve the person, not the other way around. [Ryan Coogler's direction: Community-rooted world-building.]

deliverables.title: Entitlement Coordination Blueprint: Patricia Hawkins-Morrow
deliverables.description: From the landscape map, Patricia Hawkins-Morrow now blueprints the coordination: a step-by-step pathway linking housing court, adjacent systems, and 5 other systems into a single application sequence for Arleen Belle. Extending "The Architecture of Rights", the blueprint identifies which applications must file first, which deadlines gate others, and where a single navigator could collapse 7 separate timelines into one. A right on paper and a right in practice are two different buildings. The blueprint converts legal entitlements into an operational choreography — Arleen Belle should never have to explain their situation twice. [Ryan Coogler's direction: Community-rooted world-building.]

Starting with stability, the analysis asks: what does stability actually look like for Arleen Belle — not in the abstract, but on a Tuesday morning? A kitchen is the most honest room in any building. The framework maps each dimension against current system coverage and finds that public schools addresses stability partially but ignores stability entirely. [Ryan Coogler's direction: Community-rooted world-building.]

prior_art.title: Legal Landscape Map: Patricia Hawkins-Morrow
prior_art.description: Applying "The Architecture of Rights" to Arleen Belle's case, Patricia Hawkins-Morrow identifies that Arleen Belle intersects with 7 systems simultaneously — focusing first on Medicaid. The gap analysis reveals that eligibility exists on paper across all systems, but application timelines conflict and documentation requirements between Medicaid and adjacent systems duplicate effort without sharing data. A right on paper and a right in practice are two different buildings. The map exposes 7 specific coordination failures that, if resolved, would unlock dignity simultaneously. [Ryan Coogler's direction: Community-rooted world-building.]

prior_art.title: Public Voice Installation: Zara Kalil
prior_art.description: Nobody put public art on the production plan for this dome.. But Zara Kalil brings "The Unofficial History Project" — 40+ large-scale murals documenting community histories in neighborhoods facing gentrification. Applied to Arleen Belle's dome, their practice reframes education as something the other disciplines couldn't see. A wall is either talking or it's silent. Their methodology from "Wall Rights" — Legal advocacy for artists' rights to public expression, established precedent i — produces an entirely new category of output for Arleen Belle's dome. This is the collision that makes the portfolio undeniable.

prior_art.title: dome Nourishment Protocol: Santiago Reyes-Moreno

This is the collision that makes the portfolio undeniable.

prior_art.title: Production Narrative Framework: Andre Washington
prior_art.description: From the treatment, Andre Washington architects the production narrative: how Arleen Belle's story moves through the dome pipeline. Drawing on "Docket", the framework structures the dome not as a rescue story but as an engineering problem — 7 systems that should work together and don't, and what it takes to make them. The camera doesn't have an opinion, but it has a position. The narrative architecture gives equal weight to Arleen Belle's intelligence, humor, and agency as it does to the systems that constrain them. [Ryan Coogler's direction: Community-rooted world-building.]

prior_art.title: Cost-of-Fragmentation Model: Dr. Kenji Nakamura
prior_art.description: From the fragmentation model, Dr. Kenji Nakamura builds the cost comparison: what Arleen Belle's case costs under current fragmentation vs. what it would cost under coordination. Extending "Cascade: Hospital Readmission Feedback Loops", the model shows that public schools and adjacent systems alone generate duplicate administrative costs — the same information collected twice, stored in incompatible formats, verified by different standards. Every system failure has a signature — a pattern of cascading consequences that you can map, model, and predict. The cost-of-fragmentation model makes the invisible tax visible: Arleen Belle is paying for government's failure to coordinate, in hours that could go toward stability. [Ryan Coogler's direction: Community-rooted world-building.]

prior_art.title: Dome Architecture Concept: Amara Okafor
prior_art.description: From the dimensions analysis, Amara Okafor designs the dome's architecture: a spatial and conceptual environment where Arleen Belle's 7 flourishing dimensions are structurally supported. Drawing on "Roots Kitchen Network", the concept treats every transition between housing court and adjacent systems as a design problem — not a bureaucratic one. A kitchen is the most honest room in any building. The dome's architecture ensures that pursuing housing never requires sacrificing stability. The systems serve the person, not the other way around. [Ryan Coogler's direction: Community-rooted world-building.]

prior_art.title: Entitlement Coordination Blueprint: Patricia Hawkins-Morrow

stages.focus: Map the legal landscape. Discover every entitlement. Model every system.

deliverables.title: Character Documentary Treatment: Andre Washington
deliverables.description: Drawing on "Docket", Andre Washington builds the documentary treatment around Arleen Belle's full landscape: A mother whose primary project is keeping her family together and housed. Resourceful, strategic, deeply knowledgeable a. The treatment refuses to flatten Arleen Belle into a case study. The camera doesn't have an opinion, but it has a position. The narrative structure mirrors the fragmentation — the audience experiences the same disorientation of navigating 7 disconnected systems, then the relief when coordination begins to work. [Ryan Coogler's direction: Community-rooted world-building.]

deliverables.title: Systems Fragmentation Model: Dr. Kenji Nakamura
deliverables.description: Extending "Cascade: Hospital Readmission Feedback Loops" to Arleen Belle's situation, Dr. Kenji Nakamura models the cascade: a single failure in Medicaid propagates through Medicaid, compounding costs at each handoff. The model quantifies the fragmentation tax — the hours, the duplicate applications, the missed deadlines that aren't bugs but features of systems designed to operate in isolation. Every system failure has a signature — a pattern of cascading consequences that you can map, model, and predict. The simulation shows that coordinating just 1 of 7 systems would reduce the administrative burden on Arleen Belle by the equivalent of a part-time job. [Ryan Coogler's direction: Community-rooted world-building.]

deliverables.title: Flourishing Design Package: Amara Okafor
deliverables.description: The concept becomes a complete design package. Amara Okafor delivers Arleen Belle's dome — every system, every transition, every threshold designed for dignity. Using "Roots Kitchen Network" and "Season Tables", the package specifies how rental assistance connects to adjacent systems without requiring Arleen Belle to re-prove eligibility. How the physical environment supports employment. How the schedule respects stability. A kitchen is the most honest room in any building. This is the dome. Not a concept — a deliverable. [Ryan Coogler's direction: Community-rooted world-building.]
